metadata.summary: Decide between HAProxy, Nginx, cloud LBs, and Kubernetes Ingress controllers using a structured decision matrix for production workloads.

content.title: Load Balancer Technology Selection
content.summary: Choose the right load balancer for production using a decision matrix covering HAProxy, Nginx, AWS/GCP/Azure managed LBs, and Kubernetes Ingress controllers. Selection criteria: traffic type (L4/L7), environment (bare-metal/cloud/K8s), concurrency, and operational complexity.

why.p: Picking the wrong LB forces a costly migration. HAProxy and Nginx have fundamentally different strengths — TCP performance vs. web server versatility — and cloud managed LBs trade configuration flexibility for operational simplicity. Kubernetes environments add another axis: Nginx Ingress vs. HAProxy Ingress vs. Gateway API. Getting this right upfront avoids rewrites when traffic grows or requirements change.

list.item: Starting a new service fleet and selecting the LB stack from scratch.
Migrating from a single-instance setup to a multi-backend load-balanced architecture.
Evaluating whether to use a managed cloud LB or a self-hosted solution.
Choosing a Kubernetes Ingress controller for a new cluster.
Internal service-to-service mesh routing — use a service mesh (Linkerd, Istio, Cilium) instead; LB selection does not apply.
Static-site CDN — CloudFront, Cloudflare, or Fastly serve this role; no LB configuration needed.
Single-instance dev workloads — nginx -t and docker run -p are sufficient.
Database load balancing (PgBouncer, ProxySQL, Vitess) — generic LB rules do not fit; use database-specific patterns.

section.name: decision-matrix
section.p: Use this matrix to route each scenario to the best solution before touching any configuration:

list.item: Raw TCP performance, high concurrency → HAProxy
Web server + reverse proxy combo → Nginx
Kubernetes simple HTTP routing → Nginx Ingress
Kubernetes advanced L4/L7 → HAProxy Ingress
AWS managed, auto-scaling → ALB/NLB
Multi-cloud, future-proof → Gateway API

section.name: haproxy-selection
section.p: Choose HAProxy when the workload demands raw connection throughput and fine-grained control:

list.item: TCP-heavy environments — 10-15% better raw connection handling than Nginx.
High concurrency needs — superior under extreme loads.
Fine-grained connection control — advanced retries, circuit breaking.
Native HTTP/3 and gRPC support required.
Microservices/IoT — rapid connection establishment patterns.
Bare metal/self-hosted — maximum performance control.

section.name: nginx-selection
section.p: Choose Nginx when the LB must also serve static content or act as a web server:

list.item: Web server + LB combo — versatile out-of-box.
Static content caching — built-in cache layer.
Simpler configuration — easier for basic setups.
WebSocket proxying — native support.
Path-based routing — clean HTTP routing rules.

section.name: combined-ha-pattern
section.p: For bare-metal and on-premise Kubernetes clusters, combine both: HAProxy as TCP load balancer in front of Ingress-NGINX pods. This gives L4 performance at the edge and L7 routing flexibility inside the cluster.

example.title: HAProxy + Nginx Ingress combined HA architecture
example.code: HAProxy (L4 TCP LB) | +-- Nginx Ingress Pod 1 (Node A) +-- Nginx Ingress Pod 2 (Node B) +-- Nginx Ingress Pod 3 (Node C) | +-- Backend Services
example.note: Use HAProxy as TCP load balancer in front of Ingress-NGINX for bare-metal and on-premise Kubernetes clusters.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive LB work through IaC and config templates, not direct edits to running boxes. Workflow: agent renders config from a structured input (backends, paths, TLS source, rate limits), validates with nginx -t / haproxy -c -f / ingress-nginx-validate, runs a synthetic health-check matrix in a staging environment, opens a PR. Cluster-side: prefer Ingress controller plus Gateway API CRDs over hand-edited configs. Never kubectl edit ingress from the agent — only via Git.

list.item: Set up GitHub Actions workflow from template — haiku (pattern application, simple configuration).
Design CI/CD pipeline architecture — opus (complex system design with many variables).
Write terraform code for infrastructure — sonnet (implementation with moderate complexity).
Debug failing pipeline step — sonnet (debugging and problem-solving).
Implement AIOps anomaly detection — opus (novel ML approach, complex decision).
Configure webhook and secret management — haiku (mechanical setup using checklists).

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Validation and operations tools for each LB type:

list.item: haproxy -c -f — validate HAProxy config (https://www.haproxy.org/)
nginx -t / nginx -T — validate NGINX, dump effective config (https://nginx.org/en/docs/)
kubectl ingress-nginx plugin — diagnose NGINX Ingress (https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx/kubectl-plugin/)
traefikctl (traefik binary) — Traefik validation/info (https://doc.traefik.io/traefik/)
envoy --mode validate — validate Envoy config (https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/)
awscli elbv2 / gcloud compute backend-services — cloud LB ops
mkcert / cfssl — local certs for testing (https://github.com/FiloSottile/mkcert)
vegeta / wrk2 / oha — load testing through the LB (https://github.com/tsenart/vegeta)
httpx (ProjectDiscovery) — probing TLS, headers, redirects (https://github.com/projectdiscovery/httpx)
testssl.sh — TLS endpoint hardening test (https://testssl.sh/)
keepalived / ucarp — VIP failover for active/passive HA (https://www.keepalived.org/)
HAProxy / HAProxy Enterprise — OSS/SaaS, agent-friendly via Data Plane API plus Runtime API; best raw L4 perf, dataplaneapi for dynamic config.
NGINX / NGINX Plus — OSS/SaaS, agent-friendly via Plus REST API; reverse proxy + L7, cache built-in.
Envoy / Envoy Gateway — OSS, agent-friendly via xDS API; dynamic config, native to mesh.
Traefik — OSS, agent-friendly via file/CRD providers; auto TLS via ACME, dynamic.
Caddy — OSS, agent-friendly via JSON API; easiest TLS automation.
AWS ALB / NLB / GLB — SaaS, agent-friendly via Terraform/CDK; ALB L7, NLB L4, GLB for appliances.
GCP Cloud Load Balancing — SaaS, agent-friendly; global anycast L7, regional L4.
Azure Front Door / App Gateway — SaaS, agent-friendly; Front Door global, App Gateway regional.
Cloudflare LB / Spectrum — SaaS, agent-friendly via API; global, integrated WAF.
MetalLB — OSS, agent-friendly via CRDs; bare-metal K8s LoadBalancer Service.
Kong / Tyk / APISIX — OSS/SaaS, agent-friendly via admin API; combined gateway plus LB.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common LLM mistakes when selecting and configuring load balancers:

list.item: LLMs suggest worker_connections 100000 blindly — verify against ulimit -n and kernel nofile first.
HAProxy balance algorithm choice — agents pick source for stickiness but conflate that with proper session affinity; clarify intent.
NGINX location ordering — agents emit overlapping prefixes; require disjoint or use = / ^~ modifiers.
Ingress annotation drift — nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/... vs traefik.ingress.kubernetes.io/...; LLMs mix them. Pin the controller in the prompt.
TLS cipher copy-paste — 2018 cipher lists still circulate; force the Mozilla SSL Configurator output for current year/profile.
Health endpoint in app — agents wire /healthz but the app returns 200 even when DB is down; push for deep health checks via dependency probe.
HTTP/2 + sticky cookie — NGINX has subtle interactions; verify with curl --http2 -v.
WAF rules generated by LLM frequently false-positive on legit traffic; deploy in DetectionOnly first.

rule.statement: Never replace a managed cloud LB with a self-hosted solution just to save money before you have outgrown it. Operational overhead (HA setup, patching, certificate renewal, failover testing) routinely exceeds cloud LB costs at small scale.
rule.rationale: Self-hosted LBs require keepalived or UCARP for VIP failover, manual cert renewal, and active monitoring — all of which are included in managed services. The break-even point is typically 100k+ req/s or strict compliance requirements.

rule.statement: Sticky sessions on stateless services MUST be flagged as an architectural smell before implementing them. Sticky sessions break autoscaling.
rule.rationale: Agents implementing sticky sessions on stateless services usually mask a real architectural issue (missing shared session store, missing distributed cache). Fix the root cause first.
